sentences.zh: 韓子曰：「儒以文亂法，而俠以武犯禁。」

translations.literal: Han Feizi said: "The Confucians use their writings to undermine the law, while the knights-errant use their martial prowess to violate the prohibitions."
translations.idiomatic: Han Feizi once wrote: "Confucian scholars subvert the law with their writings, and wandering knights defy authority with their swords."

sentences.zh: 二者皆譏，而學士多稱於世云。

translations.literal: Both of these are criticized, yet scholars are much praised by the world.
translations.idiomatic: Both were censured, yet scholars have always found admirers in the world.

sentences.zh: 至如以術取宰相卿大夫，輔翼其世主，功名俱著於春秋，固無可言者。

translations.literal: As for those who by craft attained the positions of chancellor, ministers, and grandees, who assisted and supported their rulers, their achievements and fame all recorded in the annals, there is nothing that needs to be said about them.
translations.idiomatic: Those who rose to become chancellors and ministers through political craft, who served as their lord's right hand, whose deeds and reputations fill the histories — they need no further comment.

sentences.zh: 及若季次、原憲，閭巷人也，讀書懷獨行君子之德，義不茍合當世，當世亦笑之。

translations.literal: As for Ji Ci and Yuan Xian, they were men of the back alleys, who read books and held fast to the virtues of the solitary-minded gentleman, whose integrity refused to compromise with their times, and their times also laughed at them.
translations.idiomatic: Then there were men like Ji Ci and Yuan Xian — common folk of the lanes and alleys who cherished their books and upheld the solitary virtues of the true gentleman. They refused on principle to bend to the age, and the age laughed at them in return.

sentences.zh: 故季次、原憲終身空室蓬戶，褐衣疏食不厭。

translations.literal: Thus Ji Ci and Yuan Xian lived their whole lives in empty rooms with bramble-woven doors, in coarse garments and meager food without complaint.
translations.idiomatic: So Ji Ci and Yuan Xian lived out their days in bare rooms behind wattle doors, clad in rough cloth and eating thin gruel, yet never once complained.

sentences.zh: 死而已四百餘年，而弟子志之不倦。

translations.literal: They have been dead for over four hundred years, yet their disciples record them without tiring.
translations.idiomatic: More than four hundred years after their deaths, their disciples still honor their memory without flagging.

sentences.zh: 今游俠，其行雖不軌於正義，然其言必信，其行必果，已諾必誠，不愛其軀，赴士之阸困，既已存亡死生矣，而不矜其能，羞伐其德，蓋亦有足多者焉。

translations.literal: Now the wandering knights, though their conduct may not conform to standards of righteousness, yet their words are always trustworthy, their actions always decisive, their promises always kept; they do not spare their own bodies, they rush to the aid of gentlemen in distress, having already risked life and death, yet they do not boast of their abilities, are ashamed to brag of their virtues — there is indeed much in them worthy of praise.
translations.idiomatic: Now consider the wandering knights. Their conduct may not conform to orthodox morality, yet when they give their word they keep it, when they commit to action they see it through, and when they make a promise they honor it with their lives. They throw themselves into the perils of others without sparing their own bodies, having stared down life and death without flinching — and yet they never boast of their abilities or trumpet their virtue. Surely there is much in them to admire.

sentences.zh: 且緩急，人之所時有也。

translations.literal: Moreover, the need for urgent help is something that comes to everyone from time to time.
translations.idiomatic: After all, desperate need is something that comes to every person sooner or later.

sentences.zh: 太史公曰：昔者虞舜窘於井廩，伊尹負於鼎俎，傅說匿於傅險，呂尚困於棘津，夷吾桎梏，百里飯牛，仲尼畏匡，菜色陳、蔡。

translations.literal: The Grand Historian said: In ancient times, Emperor Shun was trapped in a well and a granary; Yi Yin bore the burdens of the cooking vessels; Fu Yue hid at the cliff of Fu; Lu Shang was stranded at Jijin; Yi Wu was shackled; Baili Xi herded cattle; Confucius was threatened at Kuang, and his face grew gaunt at Chen and Cai.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Historian remarks: In the old days, Emperor Shun was nearly killed in a well and a granary. Yi Yin labored among the pots and cutting boards. Fu Yue hid himself at the cliffs of Fu. Lu Shang was reduced to desperate straits at Jijin. Guan Zhong wore fetters. Baili Xi fed cattle for a living. Confucius was besieged at Kuang and went hungry until his face turned sallow between Chen and Cai.

sentences.zh: 此皆學士所謂有道仁人也，猶然遭此菑，況以中材而涉亂世之末流乎？

translations.literal: These were all what scholars call men of the Way and benevolence, yet they still suffered such calamities — how much more so for those of ordinary talent who wade through the turbid currents of a disordered age?
translations.idiomatic: All of these were the very paragons that scholars call men of virtue and benevolence — yet even they suffered such calamities. How much worse, then, for ordinary people struggling through the dregs of a disordered age?

sentences.zh: 其遇害何可勝道哉！

translations.literal: The harm they encountered — how can it be fully described!
translations.idiomatic: The hardships people endure are beyond all telling!

sentences.zh: 鄙人有言曰：「何知仁義，已饗其利者為有德。」

translations.literal: The common people have a saying: "What do they know of benevolence and righteousness? Those who have enjoyed the benefits are considered virtuous."
translations.idiomatic: Common folk have a saying: "What does anyone know about benevolence and righteousness? Whoever reaps the profits is the one called virtuous."

sentences.zh: 故伯夷丑周，餓死首陽山，而文武不以其故貶王；

translations.literal: Thus Bo Yi despised the Zhou dynasty and starved to death on Mount Shouyang, yet Kings Wen and Wu were not diminished in their kingship on his account;
translations.idiomatic: Bo Yi despised the Zhou conquest and starved himself to death on Mount Shouyang, yet Kings Wen and Wu were none the less regarded as kings on that account.

sentences.zh: 跖、蹻暴戾，其徒誦義無窮。

translations.literal: Zhi and Qiao were cruel and violent, yet their followers praised their righteousness without end.
translations.idiomatic: Robber Zhi and Zhuang Qiao were violent and cruel, yet their followers sang praises to their righteousness without end.

sentences.zh: 由此觀之，「竊鉤者誅，竊國者侯，侯之門仁義存」，非虛言也。

translations.literal: Looking at it from this perspective, "He who steals a belt buckle is executed, he who steals a kingdom becomes a feudal lord, and at the lord's gate benevolence and righteousness dwell" — these are not empty words.
translations.idiomatic: Seen in this light, the old saying is no empty proverb: "Steal a belt buckle and you are executed; steal a kingdom and you are made a lord — and at the lord's gate, benevolence and righteousness take up residence."

sentences.zh: 今拘學或抱咫尺之義，久孤於世，豈若卑論儕俗，與世沈浮而取榮名哉！

translations.literal: Now those confined scholars who cling to their narrow righteousness, long isolated from the world — how can they compare with those who lower their discourse to match the vulgar, who rise and fall with the world to win glory and fame!
translations.idiomatic: Today's pedantic scholars may cling to their narrow slice of righteousness and remain alone in the world for a lifetime — but can that compare with those who lower their standards to the common level and ride the currents of the age to win glory and renown?

sentences.zh: 而布衣之徒，設取予然諾，千里誦義，為死不顧世，此亦有所長，非茍而已也。

translations.literal: Yet common men who set their giving and receiving, their promises and pledges, whose righteousness is praised a thousand li away, who face death without caring for worldly concerns — this too has its merits, it is not something trivial.
translations.idiomatic: Yet among the common people there are those whose dealings are governed by honor, whose word is celebrated a thousand li away, who face death without a second thought for worldly consequence — there is something admirable in this that should not be dismissed.

sentences.zh: 故士窮窘而得委命，此豈非人之所謂賢豪閒者邪？

translations.literal: Thus when a gentleman finds himself in desperate straits and can entrust his life to another, is this not what people call a worthy and heroic person?
translations.idiomatic: When a gentleman is cornered and desperate and finds someone willing to stake their life on his behalf — is this not the very thing people mean when they speak of a worthy hero?

sentences.zh: 誠使鄉曲之俠，予季次、原憲比權量力，效功於當世，不同日而論矣。

translations.literal: If one truly compared the local knights-errant with Ji Ci and Yuan Xian in terms of power and ability, and measured their contributions to the world, they could not be spoken of in the same breath.
translations.idiomatic: If one were to weigh the local knights against Ji Ci and Yuan Xian in terms of power and achievement, measuring their service to the age — they simply cannot be spoken of in the same breath.

sentences.zh: 要以功見言信，俠客之義又曷可少哉！

translations.literal: In sum, judging by their deeds and the trustworthiness of their words, how can the righteousness of the knights-errant be considered negligible!
translations.idiomatic: In the end, when we judge by deeds accomplished and promises kept, how can anyone dismiss the code of the wandering knights?

sentences.zh: 古布衣之俠，靡得而聞已。

translations.literal: The knights-errant of ancient times among the common people — none of them can be heard of anymore.
translations.idiomatic: Of the ancient knights-errant who rose from the common people, nothing survives to tell their tales.

sentences.zh: 近世延陵、孟嘗、春申、平原、信陵之徒，皆因王者親屬，藉於有土卿相之富厚，招天下賢者，顯名諸侯，不可謂不賢者矣。

translations.literal: In recent times, the likes of Yanling, Lord Mengchang, Lord Chunshen, Lord Pingyuan, and Lord Xinling, all relied on their kinship with royalty and drew upon the wealth and resources of their fiefs and ministerial positions to attract the worthy men of the world, making their names illustrious among the feudal lords — they cannot be called anything but worthy men.
translations.idiomatic: In more recent times, the Lord of Yanling, Lord Mengchang, Lord Chunshen, Lord Pingyuan, and Lord Xinling all drew on royal kinship and the vast wealth of their fiefs to attract the worthiest men under heaven and win renown among the feudal lords — they were, by any measure, men of worth.

sentences.zh: 比如順風而呼，聲非加疾，其埶激也。

translations.literal: It is like shouting with the wind at one's back — the voice is not louder, but the force of circumstance carries it further.
translations.idiomatic: It is like shouting downwind — the voice itself is no louder, but the force of circumstance carries it far.

sentences.zh: 至如閭巷之俠，修行砥名，聲施於天下，莫不稱賢，是為難耳。

translations.literal: But as for the knights-errant of the lanes and alleys, who cultivated their conduct and polished their reputations, whose fame spread throughout the world, whom all praised as worthy — that is truly difficult.
translations.idiomatic: But the knights of the lanes and alleys who cultivated their conduct and burnished their reputations until their fame spread across the realm and all people praised them as worthy — that is the truly difficult thing.

sentences.zh: 然儒、墨皆排擯不載。

translations.literal: Yet both the Confucians and the Mohists rejected and excluded them, and did not record them.
translations.idiomatic: Yet both the Confucian and the Mohist schools rejected and excluded them, leaving them unrecorded.

sentences.zh: 自秦以前，匹夫之俠，湮滅不見，余甚恨之。

translations.literal: Before the Qin dynasty, the knights-errant among the common men were buried and lost from sight — I deeply regret this.
translations.idiomatic: Before the Qin dynasty, the knights-errant who rose from humble origins were lost to oblivion. I find this deeply regrettable.

sentences.zh: 以余所聞，漢興有朱家、田仲、王公、劇孟、郭解之徒，雖時捍當世之文罔，然其私義廉絜退讓，有足稱者。

translations.literal: From what I have heard, since the rise of the Han there have been the likes of Zhu Jia, Tian Zhong, Wang Gong, Ju Meng, and Guo Jie, who though they sometimes ran afoul of the laws and prohibitions of the age, yet in their private righteousness, integrity, and modesty had much to commend them.
translations.idiomatic: From what I have heard, since the founding of the Han there have been men like Zhu Jia, Tian Zhong, Wang Gong, Ju Meng, and Guo Jie. Though they sometimes clashed with the laws of their day, their personal honor, integrity, and modesty were deserving of the highest praise.

sentences.zh: 名不虛立，士不虛附。

translations.literal: Their reputations were not established in vain, and the gentlemen who followed them did not do so for nothing.
translations.idiomatic: Their reputations were not built on nothing, and the men who rallied to them did not do so without reason.

sentences.zh: 至如朋黨宗彊比周，設財役貧，豪暴侵淩孤弱，恣欲自快，游俠亦丑之。

translations.literal: As for those who formed cliques and strengthened their clans through collusion, who used their wealth to enslave the poor, who as powerful bullies oppressed the orphaned and weak, who indulged their desires to please themselves — the wandering knights despised them too.
translations.idiomatic: As for those who formed factions and built up their clans through collusion, who used their wealth to exploit the poor, who bullied the helpless and the weak, who indulged their desires for their own pleasure — the wandering knights despised such people as much as anyone.

sentences.zh: 余悲世俗不察其意，而猥以朱家、郭解等令與暴豪之徒同類而共笑之也。

translations.literal: I lament that common opinion fails to examine their true intent, and carelessly lumps men like Zhu Jia and Guo Jie together with the violent and powerful, and laughs at them all alike.
translations.idiomatic: It grieves me that the world fails to understand their true motives and carelessly lumps men like Zhu Jia and Guo Jie together with violent strongmen, deriding them all as one.

sentences.zh: 魯朱家者，與高祖同時。

translations.literal: Zhu Jia of Lu was a contemporary of Emperor Gaozu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jia of Lu lived in the same era as Emperor Gaozu.

sentences.zh: 魯人皆以儒教，而朱家用俠聞。

translations.literal: The people of Lu all followed Confucian teachings, yet Zhu Jia was known for his chivalry.
translations.idiomatic: The people of Lu were all devoted to Confucian learning, but Zhu Jia won his fame through knight-errantry.

sentences.zh: 所藏活豪士以百數，其餘庸人不可勝言。

translations.literal: The heroes he sheltered and saved numbered in the hundreds, and the ordinary people beyond that were too many to count.
translations.idiomatic: The fugitives and heroes he sheltered and saved numbered in the hundreds; the ordinary folk he helped were too many to count.

sentences.zh: 然終不伐其能，歆其德，諸所嘗施，唯恐見之。

translations.literal: Yet he never boasted of his abilities or gloried in his virtue; as for those he had once helped, he feared nothing more than to encounter them again.
translations.idiomatic: Yet he never boasted of his abilities or took pride in his virtuous deeds, and of all the people he had once helped, he dreaded nothing more than running into them again.

sentences.zh: 振人不贍，先從貧賤始。

translations.literal: When he aided people in need, he always began with the poorest and most humble.
translations.idiomatic: When he gave aid to those in need, he always started with the poorest and most humble.

sentences.zh: 家無餘財，衣不完采，食不重味，乘不過軥牛。

translations.literal: His household had no surplus wealth, his clothes were not finely colored, his meals had no second dish, and his conveyance was no more than a yoked ox.
translations.idiomatic: His household had no spare wealth. His clothes bore no fine colors. His meals had no second course. His only conveyance was an ox-drawn cart.

sentences.zh: 專趨人之急，甚己之私。

translations.literal: He devoted himself wholly to the urgent needs of others, more than to his own private affairs.
translations.idiomatic: He devoted himself entirely to the urgent needs of others, putting them above his own private concerns.

sentences.zh: 既陰脫季布將軍之阸，及布尊貴，終身不見也。

translations.literal: After he had secretly rescued General Ji Bu from danger, once Ji Bu rose to honor and eminence, Zhu Jia never saw him again for the rest of his life.
translations.idiomatic: After he had secretly rescued General Ji Bu from peril, once Ji Bu rose to a position of honor, Zhu Jia never sought him out again for the rest of his life.

sentences.zh: 自關以東，莫不延頸願交焉。

translations.literal: From the Pass eastward, there was no one who did not crane their necks and desire to form a friendship with him.
translations.idiomatic: From the Pass eastward, there was no one who did not crane their neck, hoping for the chance to befriend him.

sentences.zh: 楚田仲以俠聞，喜劍，父事朱家，自以為行弗及。

translations.literal: Tian Zhong of Chu was known for his chivalry, loved the sword, served Zhu Jia as a father, and considered his own conduct inferior.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Zhong of Chu was known for his chivalry and loved swordsmanship. He served Zhu Jia with the respect due a father and considered his own conduct inferior to the master's.

sentences.zh: 田仲已死，而雒陽有劇孟。

translations.literal: After Tian Zhong died, there was Ju Meng in Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: After Tian Zhong passed away, there arose in Luoyang a man named Ju Meng.

sentences.zh: 周人以商賈為資，而劇孟以任俠顯諸侯。

translations.literal: The people of the Zhou region relied on trade for their livelihood, but Ju Meng made his name among the feudal lords through knight-errantry.
translations.idiomatic: The people of the Zhou region made their living by trade, but Ju Meng won his fame among the lords through knight-errantry.

sentences.zh: 吳楚反時，條侯為太尉，乘傳車將至河南，得劇孟，喜曰：「吳楚舉大事而不求孟，吾知其無能為已矣。」

translations.literal: When Wu and Chu rebelled, the Marquis of Tiao served as Grand Commandant, and riding a relay cart on his way to Henan, he secured Ju Meng, and said with delight: "Wu and Chu have launched a great enterprise yet did not seek out Meng — I know they are incapable of achieving anything."
translations.idiomatic: When the kingdoms of Wu and Chu rose in rebellion, the Marquis of Tiao was serving as Grand Commandant. On his way to Henan by relay carriage, he secured the allegiance of Ju Meng and exclaimed with delight: "Wu and Chu have launched their great revolt without enlisting Meng — I know now they are doomed to fail."

sentences.zh: 天下騷動，宰相得之若得一敵國云。

translations.literal: When the empire was in turmoil, for the chancellor to secure him was like gaining an entire rival state.
translations.idiomatic: When the empire was in turmoil, winning Ju Meng to one's side was said to be worth as much as conquering an enemy state.

sentences.zh: 劇孟行大類朱家，而好博，多少年之戲。

translations.literal: Ju Meng's conduct was largely similar to that of Zhu Jia, though he was fond of gambling and had many youthful pastimes.
translations.idiomatic: In conduct, Ju Meng closely resembled Zhu Jia, though he had a fondness for gambling and the diversions of young men.

sentences.zh: 然劇孟母死，自遠方送喪蓋千乘。

translations.literal: When Ju Meng's mother died, those who came from distant places to attend the funeral filled about a thousand carriages.
translations.idiomatic: When Ju Meng's mother died, mourners came from distant places to attend the funeral — the carriages numbered close to a thousand.

sentences.zh: 及劇孟死，家無餘十金之財。

translations.literal: When Ju Meng himself died, his household had not even ten pieces of gold remaining.
translations.idiomatic: When Ju Meng himself died, his household had not so much as ten pieces of gold to its name.

sentences.zh: 而符離人王孟亦以俠稱江淮之閒。

translations.literal: Wang Meng of Fuli was also celebrated for his chivalry in the region between the Yangtze and the Huai rivers.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Meng of Fuli was likewise celebrated as a knight-errant throughout the region between the Yangtze and the Huai.

sentences.zh: 是時濟南瞷氏、陳周庸亦以豪聞，景帝聞之，使使盡誅此屬。

translations.literal: At this time, the Jian clan of Jinan and Zhou Yong of Chen were also known as local strongmen; Emperor Jing heard of them and dispatched envoys to have them all executed.
translations.idiomatic: Around this time, the Jian clan of Jinan and Zhou Yong of Chen were also renowned as local strongmen. Emperor Jing heard of them and dispatched agents to have them all put to death.

sentences.zh: 其後代諸白、梁韓無辟、陽翟薛兄、陜韓孺紛紛復出焉。

translations.literal: Afterwards, Zhu Bai of Dai, Han Wubi of Liang, Xue Xiong of Yangzhai, and Han Ru of Shan emerged one after another.
translations.idiomatic: After them, new figures emerged one after another: Zhu Bai of Dai, Han Wubi of Liang, Xue Xiong of Yangzhai, and Han Ru of Shan.

sentences.zh: 郭解，軹人也，字翁伯，善相人者許負外孫也。

translations.literal: Guo Jie was from Zhi, with the courtesy name Wengbo; he was the grandson on his mother's side of the famous physiognomist Xu Fu.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Jie was a native of Zhi, with the courtesy name Wengbo. He was the maternal grandson of the famous physiognomist Xu Fu.

sentences.zh: 解父以任俠，孝文時誅死。

translations.literal: Jie's father was executed during the reign of Emperor Xiaowen on account of his knight-errant activities.
translations.idiomatic: His father had practiced knight-errantry and was executed for it during the reign of Emperor Wen.

sentences.zh: 解為人短小精悍，不飲酒。

translations.literal: Jie was short and slight in stature, but tough and capable, and did not drink wine.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Jie was short and slight in build, but tough and sharp, and he did not drink.

sentences.zh: 少時陰賊，慨不快意，身所殺甚眾。

translations.literal: In his youth he was secretive and ruthless; whenever he felt indignant, those he personally killed were very many.
translations.idiomatic: In his youth he was secretive and ruthless; whenever something displeased him, the men he personally killed were very many.

sentences.zh: 以軀借交報仇，藏命作姦剽攻，（不）休（及）［乃］鑄錢掘冢，固不可勝數。

translations.literal: He risked his body for friends to take revenge, harbored fugitives, committed robberies and assaults, and also counterfeited coins and robbed tombs — such things were beyond counting.
translations.idiomatic: He risked his life to avenge his friends, harbored fugitives, committed robberies and raids, counterfeited coins, and plundered tombs — his crimes were beyond counting.

sentences.zh: 適有天幸，窘急常得脫，若遇赦。

translations.literal: Yet by fortunate coincidence, whenever he was in dire straits he always managed to escape, as if meeting with an amnesty.
translations.idiomatic: Yet as if blessed by heaven, whenever he was cornered he always managed to slip free, or happened to fall under an amnesty.

sentences.zh: 及解年長，更折節為儉，以德報怨，厚施而薄望。

translations.literal: When Jie grew older, he changed his ways and became frugal, repaid resentment with virtue, gave generously and expected little in return.
translations.idiomatic: As Guo Jie grew older, he reformed himself and became frugal. He repaid grudges with kindness, gave generously, and expected little in return.

sentences.zh: 然其自喜為俠益甚。

translations.literal: Yet his delight in acting as a knight-errant only grew stronger.
translations.idiomatic: Yet his relish for the role of knight-errant only deepened with time.

sentences.zh: 既已振人之命，不矜其功，其陰賊著於心，卒發於睚金故云。

translations.literal: Though he had saved people's lives and did not boast of his deeds, the secretive ruthlessness ingrained in his heart would still erupt over the slightest offense.
translations.idiomatic: Though he saved men's lives without taking any credit, the old ruthless instinct was etched deep in his heart and would still flare up at the slightest provocation.

sentences.zh: 而少年慕其行，亦輒為報仇，不使知也。

translations.literal: The young men who admired his conduct would also take revenge on his behalf without letting him know.
translations.idiomatic: The young men who admired him would avenge perceived slights on his behalf, without even letting him know.

sentences.zh: 解姊子負解之勢，與人飲，使之嚼。
非其任，彊必灌之。
人怒，拔刀刺殺解姊子，亡去。
解姊怒曰：「以翁伯之義，人殺吾子，賊不得。」
棄其尸於道，弗葬，欲以辱解。
解使人微知賊處。
賊窘自歸，具以實告解。
解曰：「公殺之固當，吾兒不直。」
遂去其賊，罪其姊子，乃收而葬之。
諸公聞之，皆多解之義，益附焉。
解出入，人皆避之。
有一人獨箕倨視之，解遣人問其名姓。
客欲殺之。
解曰：「居邑屋至不見敬，是吾德不修也，彼何罪！」
乃陰屬尉史曰：「是人，吾所急也，至踐更時脫之。」
每至踐更，數過，吏弗求。
怪之，問其故，乃解使脫之。
箕踞者乃肉袒謝罪。
少年聞之，愈益慕解之行。
雒陽人有相仇者，邑中賢豪居閒者以十數，終不聽。
客乃見郭解。
解夜見仇家，仇家曲聽解。
解乃謂仇家曰：「吾聞雒陽諸公在此閒，多不聽者。
今子幸而聽解，解柰何乃從他縣奪人邑中賢大夫權乎！」
乃夜去，不使人知，曰：「且無用，（待我）待我去，令雒陽豪居其閒，乃聽之。」
解執恭敬，不敢乘車入其縣廷。
之旁郡國，為人請求事，事可出，出之；
不可者，各厭其意，然後乃敢嘗酒食。
諸公以故嚴重之，爭為用。
邑中少年及旁近縣賢豪，夜半過門常十餘車，請得解客舍養之。
及徙豪富茂陵也，解家貧，不中訾，吏恐，不敢不徙。
衛將軍為言：「郭解家貧不中徙。」
上曰：「布衣權至使將軍為言，此其家不貧。」
解家遂徙。
諸公送者出千餘萬。
軹人楊季主子為縣掾，舉徙解。
解兄子斷楊掾頭。
由此楊氏與郭氏為仇。
解入關，關中賢豪知與不知，聞其聲，爭交驩解。
解為人短小，不飲酒，出未嘗有騎。
已又殺楊季主。
楊季主家上書，人又殺之闕下。
上聞，乃下吏捕解。
解亡，置其母家室夏陽，身至臨晉。
臨晉籍少公素不知解，解冒，因求出關。
籍少公已出解，解轉入太原，所過輒告主人家。
吏逐之，跡至籍少公。
少公自殺，口絕。
久之，乃得解。
窮治所犯，為解所殺，皆在赦前。
軹有儒生侍使者坐，客譽郭解，生曰：「郭解專以姦犯公法，何謂賢！」
解客聞，殺此生，斷其舌。
吏以此責解，解實不知殺者。
殺者亦竟絕，莫知為誰。
吏奏解無罪。
御史大夫公孫弘議曰：「解布衣為任俠行權，以睚眥殺人，解雖弗知，此罪甚於解殺之。
當大逆無道。」
遂族郭解翁伯。
自是之後，為俠者極眾，敖而無足數者。
然關中長安樊仲子，槐裏趙王孫，長陵高公子，西河郭公仲，太原鹵公孺，臨淮兒長卿，東陽田君孺，雖為俠而逡逡有退讓君子之風。
至若北道姚氏，西道諸杜，南道仇景，東道趙他、羽公子，南陽趙調之徒，此盜跖居民閒者耳，曷足道哉！
此乃鄉者朱家之羞也。
太史公曰：吾視郭解，狀貌不及中人，言語不足採者。
然天下無賢與不肖，知與不知，皆慕其聲，言俠者皆引以為名。
諺曰：「人貌榮名，豈有既乎！」
於戲，惜哉！
